articles.title: A Double Shot at Copyright: SCOTUS to Weigh Worldwide Termination Rights
articles.content:
丹尼斯·克劳奇

最高法院被请求裁定国会的一项法案是否能够重新分配外国版权的所有权。在BMG Rights Management (US) LLC v. Vetter案中，案号25-1391，四家音乐出版实体由保罗·克莱门特代理，正寻求最高法院复审第五巡回法院的裁定：当作者行使《美国法典》第17编第304(c)条的终止权时，作者收回的是全球范围内的作品权利，而不仅限于美国境内。Vetter v. Resnik, 163 F.4th 951 (5th Cir. 2026)。第五巡回法院的裁决完全维持了地区法院的判决，并增加了第二项裁定：根据1909年法案，已故合著者的继承人获得的续展权同样适用于全球范围。

该合议庭驳斥了"每个国家存在多个独立版权利益"的理论，转而支持单一版权理论——即根据美国版权法授予的单一版权，然后依据《伯尔尼公约》和《世界版权公约》由各成员国"承认"。在专利法中，我们假设完全相反的情况：各国专利相互独立，美国专利在边境即终止。五份法庭之友简报支持调取案卷令状。反对简报及任何支持维特一方的法庭之友简报尚未提交。

终止权：作者往往在作品价值尚未显现或处于不利谈判地位时就出售作品。国会认定这种交易值得重新来过。根据《美国法典》第17编第304(c)条，1978年之前转让权利的作者在版权第56年获得五年窗口期，可以取消交易并收回权利。第203条对后来的转让在第35年做同样的规定。这是一项属于作者个人的道德权利，不得放弃（且只能转让给继承人）。该权利"不顾任何相反协议"而生效，因此没有出版商能够通过起草条款来规避。对本案至关重要的法定表述是，终止仅及于"根据本编产生的"权利，"丝毫不影响根据任何其他联邦法、州法或外国法产生的权利"。

维特和史密斯于1962年在巴吞鲁日创作了本案诉讼核心的歌曲《Double Shot》。由迪克·霍勒和假日乐队录制的第一版从未上榜。1966年，斯温金·梅达利翁乐队发行了管风琴版本，在《公告牌》热门100榜上达到第17位，销量突破百万张，尽管一些电台因歌词涉及饮酒而停播该曲。
articles.summary:
by Dennis Crouch

The Supreme Court has been asked to decide whether an act of Congress can reallocate ownership of foreign copyrights. In BMG Rights Management (US) LLC v. Vetter, No. 25-1391, four music publishing entities represented by Paul Clement are seeking review of the Fifth Circuit's holding that when an author exercises the termination right of 17 U.S.C. § 304(c), the author recaptures the work throughout the world and not merely in the United States. Vetter v. Resnik, 163 F.4th 951 (5th Cir. 2026). The Fifth Circuit decision affirmed the district court in full and added a second holding: the renewal interest that vests in a deceased co-author's heirs under the 1909 Act was worldwide as well.

The panel rejected the theory that there are "multiple and separate copyright interests in each country" in favor of a single copyright granted under the U.S. Copyright Act and then "recognized by member countries" pursuant to the Berne and Universal Copyright Conventions. In patent law we assume the complete opposite: national patents are independent of one another, and a U.S. patent stops at the border. Five amicus briefs support certiorari. The brief in opposition, and any amici supporting Vetter, have not yet been filed.

Termination rights: Authors often sell their work before anyone knows what it is worth or from a disadvantaged bargaining position. Congress decided that bargain deserves a do-over. Under 17 U.S.C. § 304(c), an author who transferred rights before 1978 gets a five-year window, opening 56 years into the copyright, to cancel the deal and take the rights back. Section 203 does the same at year 35 for later transfers. This is a moral right personal to the author that cannot be signed away (and only transferred to heirs). It works "notwithstanding any agreement to the contrary," so no publisher can draft around it. The key statutory statement important for this case is that termination reaches only rights that "arise under this title," and "in no way affects rights arising under any other Federal, State, or foreign laws."

Vetter and Smith wrote "Double Shot," the song at the center of this litigation, in Baton Rouge in 1962. The first recording, by Dick Holler and the Holidays, never charted. Then in 1966 the Swingin' Medallions released an organ version that reached No. 17 on the Billboard Hot 100 and sold a million copies, even after some stations pulled it over its references to drinking.
